title: Scientific opinion is split on the effectiveness of anti-amyloid Alzheimer’s drugs
html_body:
Biotechnology, drug discovery and pharma |
View this email in your browser Wednesday 22 April 2026

Hello Nature readers,
This week, we hear that CAR-T-cell therapy for autoimmune disease is on track for imminent approval, consider if NMR might be coming back in fashion and learn that hardly anyone responds well to off-label cancer treatments.
Experts have criticised the Cochrane review for grouping two recently approved dementia drugs with other failed treatments to analyse the effectiveness of anti-amyloid treatments. (Thomas Deerinck, NCMIR/Science Photo Library)

Split opinion on anti-amyloid drugs

A review published by Cochrane, an international group of health researchers, has concluded that the benefits of drugs for Alzheimer’s that target amyloid plaques in the brain are negligible. But some experts have criticised the review for lumping drugs together that work differently and painting them all with the same brush; particularly Leqembi and Kisunla, which were recently approved by the US Food and Drug Administration. “They took some of the rotten ingredients and mixed it in with the fresh food, and the result is a stinky stew,” says neurologist Jason Karlawish.

The New York Times | 11 min read
 Reference: Cochrane review

NMR is coming back into vogue

Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) could be on the brink of a renaissance among drug developers. For decades, other techniques that assess the structure of drugs — such as X-ray crystallography and, more recently, cryo-electron microscopy — have overshadowed NMR because of its complexity and slowness. But over the past five years, a suite of contract research organizations (which manage research for drug companies) have been founded with the aim of bringing NMR back into the limelight as the technology improves, to help drug discovery.

Meanwhile, NMR has been used to uncover a completely new conformational state in a group of proteins strongly linked with cancer progression. The newly discovered ‘invisible fold’ in kinases could help make kinase inhibitors more selective. “Conformational dynamics in drug discovery are critical and underappreciated,” says venture capitalist Bob Copeland. “This work puts them front and center.”

Nature Biotechnology | 4 min read & Nature Biotechnology | 10 min read

Image of the week
Researchers have imaged every single cell in the body of a newborn mouse, using a custom fluorescence microscope, high-speed cameras and an atlas of the mouse body onto which they mapped data. “Since Robert Hooke first described cells in 1665, more than 360 years have passed,” says molecular biologist Hiroki Ueda, who led the research. “Yet no one had directly visualized all cells in mammalian organs or in the whole body of a newborn mammal at single-cell resolution.” They also mapped responses to drug toxicity at a single-cell scale. (Nature Methods | 3 min read)
Reference: Cell paper (Hiroki Ueda)

Features and opinion

Off-label cancer drugs don’t often work

The results of an almost decade-long trial have shown that ‘off-label’ cancer therapies — those that are used for tumours other than the ones they’re approved for, often as a last resort — aren't very effective in most cases. There were some positive results, says oncologist and study co-author Emile Voest: around 7% of people in the study responded well to an off-label treatment, some of whom can be considered ‘cured’. But it’s “humbling to see that 93% of the patients did not benefit that much” yet many still experienced side effects, he says. “We have an obligation to really share that data”, because the effects of off-label treatments aren’t usually reported.

Nature Podcast | 27 min listen
 Subscribe to the Nature Podcast on Apple Podcasts, Spotify or YouTube Music, or use the RSS feed.

CAR-T for autoimmunity is on track for 2027

“We are approaching the first regulatory approval of a CAR-T-cell therapy for autoimmune disease, anticipated within the next year, followed by several more through into 2028,” write translational scientists and company heads Gwendolyn Binder and Raphaël Ognar. The hope is that these will improve on standard therapies for autoimmune diseases, which tend to rely on the broad modulation of the immune system and the use of corticosteroids, and rarely cure people. But proof-of-concept studies of CAR-T cells produced outside the body need to translate into solid later-stage clinical data, say the authors. Exploring the new generation of CAR-T cells produced inside the body could help reduce costs and complexity to help scale this therapeutic approach, they add.

Nature Reviews Drug Discovery | 11 min read
Read more about the twists and turns of CAR-T-cell therapy for autoimmune diseases so far in Nature Reviews Drug Discovery. (76 min read)

How to bring bespoke gene editing to all

In February, the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) proposed a radical rethink of how to bring personalized CRISPR therapies, such as the one that saved the life of baby KJ Muldoon last year. Rather than treat each effort as a new drug — which means years of testing at the cost of many millions — drug companies would be able to treat many people with different mutations as part of a single clinical trial. Biomedical researcher Fyodor Urnov and Sadik Kassim, a biotechnology executive and scientist, advise how the FDA can make the most of this plan by ramping its resources, engaging with patient advocates and ensuring that supply chains, insurers and manufacturing capacity are all in place.

Nature | 13 min read

Review of the month
(Fayad, Z.A., Hirten, R.P., Nadkarni, G.N. et al./Nat. Rev. Drug Discov.)

Wearable technology for clinical trials

Researchers analysed 1,021 trials registered between 2001 and 2025 that used data from wearable devices. Adhesive patches, mostly for glucose monitoring, were the most common. But so far, only one use — a digital mobility measure used in clinical trials for the genetic disorder Duchenne muscular dystrophy — has been formally approved by a regulator. (86 min read | Nature Reviews Drug Discovery)

Quote of the week

“This is nature doing its thing, man. If you could go out and willy nilly change the surface of this molecule, you could reprogram it to target RAS.”

Chemical biologist Gregory Verdine looked to nature to find the best way to target RAS mutant proteins, which occur in some aggressive cancers as have been, until recently, undruggable. Verdine found that rather than trying to grab the RAS protein's smooth surface with a small molecule, he could reprogram a complex of three proteins. (STAT | 20 min read)

Mosquitos are a bad omen at the best of times, but mosquitoes in Iceland? Really bad. Until now, Iceland was the only Arctic nation which wasn't plagued by mosquitos in the summer. But global warming, which is happening four times as fast in the Arctic than the global average, is rapidly changing the environment and therefore its inhabitants.

Please send good bug repellent recommendations — and your feedback — to trbriefing@nature.com.

Thanks for reading,
Laura Woodrow, editor, Nature Briefing: Translational Research
With contributions by Flora Graham & Jacob Smith

summary.overview: 이슈는 알츠하이머 치료제 효과에 대한 분열된 학계의 견해와 함께, NMR 기술 부활, 오프라벨 암 치료제의 한계, CAR-T 치료법의 임상 전망, 맞춤형 유전자 편집 기술 확산 등 주요 과학 소식을 다룹니다.
covered_item_titles: 분열된 의견, 항아밀로이드 알츠하이머 치료제 효과
NMR, 약물 개발 분야에서 다시 주목받다
오프라벨 암 치료제, 대부분 효과 미흡
CAR-T 치료법, 자가면역 질환 치료로 2027년 상용화 전망
맞춤형 유전자 편집 기술, 보편화를 위한 FDA의 새로운 접근

article_briefings.title: 분열된 의견, 항아밀로이드 알츠하이머 치료제 효과
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 코크라네 연구소의 리뷰는 알츠하이머 치료제의 효과가 미미하다고 결론 내렸으나, 일부 전문가들은 리뷰의 결론에 비판을 제기하고 있습니다.

article_briefings.title: NMR, 약물 개발 분야에서 다시 주목받다
article_briefings.briefing_ko: NMR 기술이 약물 구조 분석 분야에서 다시 주목받고 있으며, X선 결정학과 냉전자현미경의 우위를 넘어서고자 합니다.

article_briefings.title: 오프라벨 암 치료제, 대부분 효과 미흡
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 10년에 걸친 시험 결과, 오프라벨 암 치료제는 대부분 효과가 없으며, 일부 긍정적 결과가 있었으나 전체적인 성과는 제한적입니다.

article_briefings.title: CAR-T 치료법, 자가면역 질환 치료로 2027년 상용화 전망
article_briefings.briefing_ko: CAR-T 치료법이 자가면역 질환 치료로 2027년 내 규제 승인을 앞두고 있으며, 이후 2028년까지 여러 치료법이 상용화될 전망입니다.

article_briefings.title: 맞춤형 유전자 편집 기술, 보편화를 위한 FDA의 새로운 접근
article_briefings.briefing_ko: FDA가 맞춤형 CRISPR 치료법을 보편화하기 위해 각각의 치료법을 신약으로 간주하는 대신 새로운 규제 체계를 제안하고 있습니다.
